title: Assignment 2 Students
body:
Homework 2 Python Application Using the monthly return data from “Data for HW2.xlsx” answer the following questions. All returns are in excess of the risk-free rate. The data corresponds to monthly returns of the market factor (Mkt), the Small minus Big factor (SMB), the High minus Low factor (HML), the RMW (Robust Minus Weak) factor long robust operating profitability firms and short weak operating profitability firms, and the CMA (Conservative Minus Aggressive) factor long conservative investment firms and short aggressive investment firms. In the last column, (EW) corresponds to an equally weighted portfolio of all factors described. The return time-series spans the sample period from 1st July 1990 to 1st February 2023. You will use this data to answer the following questions.

• Some useful Python function to know: o Read an Excel file named "file_name.xlsx" into a DataFrame: df = pd.read_excel("file_name.xlsx") o Convert the date column to datetime format: df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Date'], format='%Y%m') o Set the 'Date' column as the index of the DataFrame: df.set_index('Date', inplace=True) o Get the integer location of the date '1998-06-01' in the DataFrame index: idx = df.index.get_loc('1998-06-01') o Select a range of rows from start_idx to end_idx, inclusive of end_idx: df.iloc[start_idx:end_idx + 1] o Print a number or a variable “var”: print(var) o Prints the number of rows in the DataFrame df: print(len(df))

Question 1 (16pts): For the first question, you will use the monthly excess returns of the market factor (Mkt). Your objective is to estimate the Garch model (as shown in class) using an estimation window of 5 years of monthly returns (60 months) starting on 1st July 1990 and finishing on 1st June 1995 (i.e., this period covers exactly 5*12 = 60 monthly observations). Using the code provided for Garch, you will estimate the Garch model using the estimation window described above to obtain the Garch estimated parameters 𝛼 and β and answer the following question: What is the out-ofsample conditional variance 𝜎𝑡2 for Mkt monthly excess return forecasted by Garch for 1st July 1995 (i.e., that is for the month of June 1995) expressed in percentage? Round your answer to the 4th decimal place. If you get 0.09568 % enter 0.0957 without % sign in the answer box.

Requirement for Questions 1 & 2: • Ensure the positiveness of 𝜎𝑡2 when forecasting conditional variance based on the following dynamics: 𝜎𝑡2 = np.abs( 𝜎 2 + 𝛼 ( ( 𝑟𝑡−1 -𝜇)2 − 𝜎 2 ) + β (𝜎𝑡−1 − 𝜎 2) ) where np.abs(.) is numpy function to take the absolute value.  • When forecasting 𝜎𝑡2 out-of-sample all quantities should be computed using information prior to 1st July 1995 including 𝛼, β, 𝜇, 𝜎 2 , and 𝑟𝑡−1 where 𝑟𝑡−1 denotes the Mkt excess return on month t-1.

Question 2 (40pts): Building on your previous answer and the code you developed, you will in Question 2 implement a volatility managed trading strategy based on Mkt out-of-sample, just like what a trader or an asset manager would do. The trading period for the strategy will start on the 1st July 1995 and finish at the end of the sample on 1 st February 2023. For each month during this period (1st July 1995, 1st August 1995, etc…), you will need to estimate recursively the Garch model on Mkt excess returns using a fixed rolling window of 60 monthly observations covering the period prior to that month. The trading algorithm is as follows. Using the conditional variance 𝜎𝑡2 for Mkt on month t forecasted out-of-sample by the Garch model using the information from t-60 to t-1, you 𝜎2 will invest 𝑤𝑡 = in the Mkt excess returns on month t where 𝜎 2 is the sample variance of 𝜎𝑡2

monthly Mkt returns estimated using the same fixed rolling window from t-60 to t-1 used to obtain the Garch parameters and forecast 𝜎𝑡2 . The excess return of the volatility managed strategy on month t is thus 𝑟𝑡𝑉𝑜𝑙 = 𝑤𝑡 𝑟𝑡 where 𝑤𝑡 has been calculated using information up to t-1. Given the monthly excess returns of the volatility managed trading strategy and Mkt for the period from 1st July 1995 to 1st February 2023, answer the following question: What is the difference between the average annualized excess return of the volatility managed strategy and the Mkt in percentage? In other words, by how much in annual units does the volatility managed strategy outperforms the Mkt on average? Round your answer to the 2nd decimal place. If you get 2.112 % enter 2.11 without % sign in the answer box.

Question 3 (20pts): Your next task is to pitch an asset mix to a new client that wants to invest in an optimal equity portfolio made of the market, SMB, HML, RMW, and CMA factors. After a few meetings, you have gathered the following information. The client wants the optimal portfolio to have an annual excess return equals to that of the EW portfolio of the factors. Moreover, he wants to have a maximum of 80% of the portfolio invested in the non-market factors (SMB, HML, RMW, and CMA). He does not want to short any of the factors and want to have a maximum weight of 50% invested in the market and 30% in the remaining factors. Given your client constraints, design an optimal asset mix. For this, you will use the in-sample data to estimate each factor expected excess returns, variance and covariances as well as the EW portfolio return used to define the client target portfolio return. The in-sample period starts on 1st July 1990 and ends on 1st December 2009. For simplicity, assume that in-sample means and variances-covariances are good forecasts of the future. For the optimization, initialize all the weights to 20%. (i) What is the optimal weight in % invested in the market? (ii) what is the optimal weight in % invested in SMB? (iii) What is the optimal weight in % invested in HML? (iv) What is the optimal weight in % invested in RMW? (v) What is the optimal weight in % invested in CMA? Round your answer to the 2nd decimal place. If you get 2.112 % enter 2.11 without % sign in the answer box.

Armed with your optimal asset mix, you want now to analyze the optimal portfolio performance. To this end, you will use the EW portfolio as benchmark.  Question 4 (12pts): For both the optimal and benchmark portfolios, you first want to investigate the following measures of performance in-sample using the same in-sample period as the previous question: (i) Annualized sample average excess return in %, (ii) Annualized sample volatility in %, (iii) Annualized Sharpe ratio, (iv) probability of negative returns in %, (v) Maximum drawdown in %, (vi) VaR 95 annualized and in %. In the Moodle answers, enter in i.1 your answer for (i) for the optimal portfolio and in i.2 your answer for (i) for the benchmark. Do the same for all measures. Round your answer to the 2nd decimal place and do not include the % sign for the measures expressed in %.

Question 5 (12pts): For both the optimal and benchmark portfolios, you now want to investigate the out-of-sample performance of the optimal portfolio. For this use the optimal weight found in the previous question. Using these weights, you will analyze the performance of the portfolio out-ofsample using the period from 1st January 2010 and to 1st February 2023. Using this period, you will now compute the same measures as the previous question, that is: (i) Annualized sample average excess return in %, (ii) Annualized sample volatility in %, (iii) Annualized Sharpe ratio, (iv) probability of negative returns in %, (v) Maximum drawdown in %, (vi) VaR 95 annualized and in %. In the Moodle answers, enter in i.1 your answer for (i) for the optimal portfolio and in i.2 your answer for (i) for the benchmark. Do the same for all measures. Round your answer to the 2nd decimal place and do not include the % sign for the measures expressed in %.
